title: Demo - Finder Methods in Product API

content.problemStatement:
Run the code, API tester will get open.
Go to POST endpoint and add below data :
```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Laptop",
  "brand": "Dell",
  "supplierEmail": "test@gmail.com"
}
```

Then test GET Requests using:
```sss
GET /products
GET /products/name/Laptop
GET /products/brand/Dell
```
Observe the Response data

As you can see in this API, we are retrieving products not just by ID, but based on **specific fields like name and brand**. \
These methods used to search/filter data are called Finder Methods.

---'

**Where Finder Methods are written** \
Look at your `ProductRepository`:
```java
public List findByName(String name) {
    return products.stream()
            .filter(p -> p.getName().equalsIgnoreCase(name))
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
}

public List findByBrand(String brand) {
    return products.stream()
            .filter(p -> p.getBrand().equalsIgnoreCase(brand))
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
}
```
**Here:**
- `findByName()` → finds products by name
- `findByBrand()` → finds products by brand

These are your custom finder methods

---

**How it works internally**

**When a request comes like:**
```sss
GET /products/name/Laptop
```
**Step-by-step flow:** 
1. Controller receives request
2. Calls service:
```java
service.getByName(name)
```
3. Service calls repository:
```java
repository.findByName(name)
```
4. Repository filters data using:
```java
stream().filter(...)
```
5. Matching products are returned

---

**Controller usage**
```java
@GetMapping("/products/name/{name}")
public ResponseEntity> getByName(@PathVariable String name) {
    return ResponseEntity.ok(service.getByName(name));
}
```
**Same for brand:**
```java
@GetMapping("/products/brand/{brand}")
```


---

**Key Understanding** \
Finder methods are used to:
- Search data
- Filter data
- Retrieve specific records
